attributes.stem:
A 51-year-old woman presents to her General Practitioner with a 12-month history of progressive lower back pain. She describes a sharp, shooting pain that radiates down her right posterior thigh, but denies any weakness or numbness of her lower extremities. She reports the pain is particularly bad after sitting for long periods at work and that the pain is acutely worsened when coughing or sneezing. She has been using paracetamol and ibuprofen but without relief. She denies bowel or bladder symptoms. Her medical history includes depression, for which she takes duloxetine. She has no personal history of malignancy and denies IV drug use.

On examination, the neurological examination is unremarkable but there is a positive straight leg raise.

A previous MRI spine showed an L5/S1 disc bulge.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is presenting with neuropathic pain secondary to a herniated disc. Neuropathic pain may be described as pins and needles, tingling, burning, stinging or shooting. It is caused by injury or disease to the somatosensory nervous system - which may include the central or peripheral nervous system. Neuropathic pain will often be perceived to follow the distribution of the affected neural pathway.

In this case, the herniated disc between L5 and S1 is likely compressing some spinal roots, leading to the neuropathic symptoms described of shooting pain in the region of L5 and S1, with a positive straight leg raise.

The first-line management of neuropathic pain encompasses a variety of agents:

  • Gabapentinoids (pregabalin and gabapentin) are the preferred agent for the management of acute neuropathic pain. They have a rapid onset of action and are therefore preferred to other options.

  • Tricyclic anti-depressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline) and selective serotonin noradrenaline reuptake inhibitors (duloxetine, venlafaxine) have also been shown to be effective in reducing neuropathic pain. However, these medications should be avoided in patients already taking serotonergic medications (such as sertraline) due to the risk of serotonin syndrome. In this patient, additional antidepressants should be avoided as she is already taking duloxetine.

Second-line agents for neuropathic pain include the following:

  • Tramadol has consistently been shown to be effective in safely managing neuropathic pain. However, due to its adverse effects, including serotonin toxicity, it remains a second-line option.

  • Topical treatment (lidocaine and capsaicin patches) has some limited data on efficacy in the management of neuropathic pain, however, it is not currently available on the PBS and is therefore prohibitively expensive for patients.

Opioids such as oxycodone should be avoided due to the lack of efficacy in neuropathic pain. While strong opioids should be reserved for neuropathic pain that is refractory to the above management.
learningPoints: Gabapentinoids and antidepressants (TCA/SNRIs) are first-line for the management of neuropathic pain
references: Murnion BP. Neuropathic pain: current definition and review of drug treatment. Aust Prescr 2018;41:60–3.

Finnerup NB, Attal N, Haroutounian S, McNicol E, Baron R, Dworkin RH, et al. Pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet Neurol. 2015 Feb;14(2):162-73.
